title: Ticketmaster — System Design
description: Designing a ticketing system for high-demand events, where a fixed inventory of seats meets a flash crowd of buyers the instant tickets drop. The hard problem is correctness under massive concurrency: never sell the same seat twice, never oversell, and keep it fair — while most of the crowd will leave empty-handed. We build from a naive 'buy' call toward seat locking, a waiting room, expiring holds, and sharded hot-event isolation.

functional: Browse events and view a live seat map with availability.
Reserve specific seats, holding them briefly while the user checks out.
Complete purchase, converting a hold into a confirmed order with payment.
Release seats automatically if the hold expires before checkout.
nonFunctional: Never sell the same seat twice; never oversell inventory.
Fairness: order of access should be roughly first-come under a flash crowd.
Handle extreme, spiky concurrency at on-sale (1M+ users for thousands of seats).
Low latency for browse/reserve so the experience stays usable under load.
Resist bots and scalpers grabbing inventory unfairly.
Graceful degradation: shed or queue load rather than collapse or oversell.

capacity.label: Concurrent buyers at on-sale
capacity.note: A popular tour drop draws orders of magnitude more buyers than seats.

capacity.label: Inventory per event
capacity.note: Fixed and scarce — the contention is buyers-per-seat, not raw volume.

capacity.label: Reserve attempts/sec
capacity.note: Bursts in the first seconds; most attempts must fail fast and fairly.

capacity.label: Hold duration
capacity.note: Long enough to check out, short enough to recirculate unsold seats.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Browsing and seat-map polling vastly outnumber successful reservations.

capacity.label: Correctness target
capacity.note: Correctness dominates; we'd rather reject than oversell.

api.description: Return the seat map with availability. Served from a read model / cache, eventually consistent.

Atomically reserve specific seats (or a GA quantity), creating a short-lived hold. For high-demand events a valid admission token from the waiting room is required, so only admitted users can reserve. Idempotency-Key dedupes retries.
diagram: participant L as Seat Lock Store
C->>R: POST reservations (seats, admission token, key)
R->>R: validate admission token (signed, not expired, bound to user)

api.description: Convert a valid hold into a confirmed order via payment. Idempotent.

Join the waiting room for a high-demand on-sale; returns a signed admission token bound to the user. The token is 'waiting' until admitted, then accepted by the reservations endpoint within its admission window.
Poll (or subscribe to) the waiting-room position; when admitted, the token becomes usable to reserve within its admission window.

dataModel.name: seats
dataModel.note: One row per physical seat; the unit of contention. Status transitions are the heart of correctness. Reserved-seating only; general admission uses ga_inventory below.

dataModel.name: holds
dataModel.note: A short-lived reservation over one or more seats, with an expiry. The expiry worker reclaims stale holds.

dataModel.name: order_seats
dataModel.note: Join table linking an order to its seats (preferred over a seat_ids array; enforces a seat belongs to one order).

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: A purchase produced by checkout, modeled as a state machine rather than a single confirmed row.

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: Durable record of each charge/refund attempt against an order, for idempotency and reconciliation.

dataModel.name: ga_inventory
dataModel.note: General-admission pools: a remaining-count per pool, decremented atomically (no per-seat rows).

dataModel.name: queue_tokens
dataModel.note: Waiting-room admission tokens for fairness during on-sale spikes; bound to identity and time-boxed so they actually gate load and abuse.

patterns.name: Distributed lock / seat locking
patterns.what: Acquire an exclusive, short-lived lock on each contended resource (seat) before mutating it.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple clients race to claim the same scarce item.

patterns.name: Optimistic concurrency control
patterns.what: Read a version, write only if it's unchanged (compare-and-set), retry on conflict.
patterns.whenToUse: High contention where holding pessimistic locks would serialize everything.

patterns.name: Virtual waiting room
patterns.what: Admit users from a queue at a controlled rate so the core system sees bounded load.
patterns.whenToUse: Flash crowds far exceeding capacity, where fairness and overload protection matter.

patterns.name: TTL / lease expiry
patterns.what: Give holds a time-to-live and reclaim them automatically when it lapses.
patterns.whenToUse: Reserved-but-not-bought resources that must recirculate if abandoned.

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: Dedupe retried reserve/checkout calls so a user doesn't double-hold or double-buy.
patterns.whenToUse: State-changing calls over an unreliable network under load.

patterns.name: Read model / cache
patterns.what: Serve seat maps and availability from a fast, eventually-consistent projection, not the inventory primary.
patterns.whenToUse: Very read-heavy browse traffic over a write-contended store.

patterns.name: Load shedding / admission control
patterns.what: Reject or queue excess load early so the protected core stays correct.
patterns.whenToUse: Demand can exceed capacity by orders of magnitude.

patterns.name: Sharding by key
patterns.what: Partition inventory by event so one hot event's contention is isolated from others.
patterns.whenToUse: A few hot events would otherwise overwhelm shared infrastructure.

steps.title: 1. Naive 'Buy a Seat' (the baseline)
steps.description: Start with the simplest path: the client posts a purchase for a seat, the API checks availability and writes 'sold'. With one buyer this works; the whole design exists because thousands hit the same seat in the same millisecond.
steps.decisionPrompt: Two requests read seat A12 as available at the same instant. What happens?

concepts.definition: Two operations interleave so both see 'available' and both write 'sold'.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the central hazard — a check-then-act without atomicity double-sells the seat.

concepts.definition: Many actors competing for the same resource at once.
concepts.whyItMatters: Ticketing is buyers-per-seat contention, not raw throughput; that shapes every choice.

talkingPoints: State that correctness (no double-sell/oversell) dominates latency or availability here.
Name the check-then-act race immediately — it motivates locking.

traps.trap: Check availability, then write sold as two separate steps
traps.why: Concurrent buyers both pass the check and both write sold — the seat is sold twice.
traps.instead: Make claim atomic (a conditional update / lock), not check-then-act.

bottlenecks.issue: Unbounded concurrent writes to the same seat row.

strong: Spots the double-sell race without prompting.
Frames the problem as contention/correctness, not throughput.
weak: Assumes the DB write 'just handles' concurrency.

whyNow: A concrete baseline makes the locking and queueing that follow obvious responses to a real failure.

steps.title: 2. Browse: Seat Map Read Model
steps.description: Browsing and seat-map polling dwarf purchases. Serve availability from a read model / cache (and static seat maps from a CDN), accepting slight staleness — the authoritative check happens at reserve time anyway.
steps.decisionPrompt: Can the seat map a user sees be slightly stale? What must NOT be stale?

concepts.definition: A projection of inventory that lags the source of truth slightly.
concepts.whyItMatters: Browse traffic is enormous; serving it from the contended primary would melt it.

concepts.definition: Availability shown is a hint; the real check is the atomic reserve.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets reads be cheap and stale while keeping the purchase correct.

deepDives.title: Keeping the seat map fresh enough
points: Static seat geometry (the map image/layout) is immutable — cache it hard on a CDN.
Availability overlays change fast — push updates to the cache; tolerate seconds of lag.
Show 'available' optimistically; the reserve call is the source of truth and may 409.

traps.trap: Serving the live seat map straight from the inventory primary
traps.why: Millions of pollers on the write-contended store crush it exactly when it's most loaded.
traps.instead: Serve from a read model/cache; reserve against the primary.

traps.trap: Promising the displayed availability is exact
traps.why: Under a flash crowd it's stale by the time the user clicks.
traps.instead: Treat it as a hint and confirm atomically at reserve.

strong: Separates immutable seat geometry from fast-changing availability.
Accepts stale reads but keeps the write authoritative.
weak: Tries to make the seat map strongly consistent.

recap.before: Every view hit the inventory primary.
recap.after: Browse is cheap and cached; the primary is reserved for claims.
recap.newRisk: Displayed availability is stale — reserve must be the real check.

whyNow: Offloading reads protects the primary before we pour on-sale write load onto it.

steps.title: 3. Atomic Reservation with Seat Locking
steps.description: Replace check-then-act with an atomic claim: acquire a short-lived exclusive lock on each requested seat before marking it held. If any seat is already locked or sold, the whole reservation fails fast with 409. Idempotency keys make retries safe.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you atomically grab 3 specific adjacent seats, all-or-nothing, under contention?

concepts.definition: An exclusive, expiring claim on a resource across processes (e.g. a row lock or a Redis lock with TTL).
concepts.whyItMatters: Serializes the claim on each seat so only one buyer wins.

concepts.definition: A request for several seats succeeds only if all are obtainable.
concepts.whyItMatters: Partial holds strand seats and frustrate users; acquire in a fixed order to avoid deadlock.

options.name: Lock store with TTL (e.g. Redis) (default)
pros: Very fast claims; locks auto-expire so a crashed client can't strand seats forever.
Keeps lock contention off the inventory primary.
cons: Lock store and inventory can diverge on failure — needs reconciliation/fencing.

options.name: Conditional UPDATE in the DB (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE / CAS)
pros: Single source of truth; no lock/inventory divergence.
Strong correctness with no extra system.
cons: Row locks under extreme contention serialize and can thrash the primary.
Hot rows become a bottleneck on the biggest drops.
diagram: Reserve -->|UPDATE seat SET held WHERE available| Inventory

flows.name: Two buyers race for A12
flows.note: Atomic lock means exactly one wins; the other gets 409.

traps.trap: Locking seats one by one without a consistent order
traps.why: Two multi-seat requests can each hold one of the other's seats — deadlock.
traps.instead: Acquire locks in a fixed global order (e.g. sorted seat id) so cycles can't form.

traps.why: A client that crashes mid-reservation strands those seats forever.
traps.instead: Give every lock a TTL; pair with the hold expiry worker to reclaim.

failureDrills.scenario: Reservation service crashes after locking seats but before writing the hold.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Seats become available again shortly, not stranded.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lock TTL expires; idempotent retry re-acquires or the expiry worker reclaims.

strong: Makes the claim atomic and all-or-nothing with ordered acquisition.
Gives locks a TTL and explains the lock-store vs DB-CAS tradeoff.

recap.before: Concurrent buyers could double-sell a seat.
recap.after: Each seat is claimed atomically; exactly one buyer wins, others 409.
recap.newRisk: Held-but-unbought seats can be stranded — they need expiry.

whyNow: Atomic claiming is the core correctness guarantee everything else builds on.

steps.title: 4. Holds & TTL Expiry
steps.description: A reservation creates a hold with a short TTL so a user can check out. If they abandon it, an expiry mechanism releases the seats and the locks so inventory recirculates. Without this, scarce seats leak out of the market.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user reserves 2 seats and walks away. How and when do those seats come back?

concepts.definition: The window during which reserved seats are off the market for one buyer.
concepts.whyItMatters: Long enough to check out, short enough to recirculate abandoned seats during a hot drop.

concepts.definition: Reclaiming holds whose TTL lapsed, by a sweeper and/or lazy check.
concepts.whyItMatters: Determines how fast scarce seats return to availability.

options.name: Sweeper worker + lazy check (default)
pros: Background worker reaps expired holds promptly; lazy check at access catches stragglers.
Bounded staleness even if the worker lags.
cons: Two mechanisms to reason about; clock skew must be handled.

options.name: Rely on lock-store TTL only
cons: Inventory 'held' state and the hold row aren't cleaned, drifting from reality.
No clean event to refresh the availability cache.

traps.trap: Releasing the lock but not the hold/inventory state
traps.why: Seat shows 'held' forever in the DB even though the lock is gone — it's lost inventory.
traps.instead: Expiry must release lock, hold row, and inventory status together, then refresh the cache.

traps.trap: Trusting wall-clock expiry across services with skew
traps.why: A seat may be released early or late if clocks disagree.
traps.instead: Anchor TTL to a single authoritative clock/store and reap from there.

failureDrills.scenario: The expiry worker is down for several minutes during a hot drop.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Abandoned seats still recirculate, just a bit slower; nothing is double-sold.
failureDrills.mitigation: Lazy check at reserve time treats an expired hold as available; the worker catches up on restart.

strong: Releases lock + hold + inventory atomically and refreshes the cache.
Combines a sweeper with a lazy check for robustness.
weak: Assumes the expiry worker never lags.

recap.before: Reserved seats could be stranded by abandonment.
recap.after: Abandoned holds expire and seats recirculate automatically.
recap.newRisk: Expiry timing trades user grace period against recirculation speed.

whyNow: With locking in place, leaked inventory is the next way scarce seats are lost.

steps.title: 5. Virtual Waiting Room (fairness + overload protection)
steps.description: At on-sale, 1M+ users hit a few thousand seats. A virtual waiting room admits users to the booking core at a controlled rate, giving rough first-come fairness and protecting the reserve path from a thundering herd.
steps.decisionPrompt: A million users arrive in the first second. How do you stay fair and keep the core alive?

concepts.definition: A queue that issues positions and admits users to the real system at a metered rate.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds the load the contended core ever sees, and makes ordering visible/fair.

concepts.definition: Letting in only as many users as the core can safely serve.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents collapse; converts an overload into an orderly wait.

deepDives.title: Fairness vs. gaming
points: Assign a position at join time; admit in position order at a rate the core sustains.
Tie tokens to identity/device to blunt users multiplying their queue entries.
Show estimated wait so users don't hammer refresh (which would re-create the herd).

traps.trap: No waiting room — let everyone hit reserve directly
traps.why: The lock store and primary are swamped; latency explodes and fairness is whoever has the fastest connection/bot.
traps.instead: Meter admission through a queue so the core sees bounded, ordered load.

traps.trap: Admitting far more users than there are seats with no expectation-setting
traps.why: Most admitted users still fail to get seats and retry-storm.
traps.instead: Admit at a rate matched to seat availability and communicate position/odds.

failureDrills.scenario: The waiting room itself gets the flash crowd.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It absorbs load without admitting a herd downstream.
failureDrills.mitigation: The queue is a lightweight, horizontally scalable buffer (e.g. token issuance + a sorted store); only admission rate touches the core.

strong: Introduces admission control to bound core load and provide fairness.
Ties queue position to identity and sets expectations.
weak: Sends the whole crowd at the reserve path.
Treats the waiting room as cosmetic.

recap.before: The reserve path faced the full flash crowd.
recap.after: Admission is metered and roughly fair; the core sees bounded load.
recap.newRisk: The queue must itself scale and resist gaming.

whyNow: Correct locking still collapses under 1M concurrent claims — the herd must be shaped before it arrives.

steps.title: 6. Checkout & Payment Integration
steps.description: Checkout drives an order state machine (pending_payment -> confirmed, with cancelled/refund_pending branches), not just a charge plus an order row. It re-verifies (and briefly renews) the hold, records a payment attempt, charges the idempotent payment system, then flips seats from held to sold via inventory CAS. Expired holds fail cleanly; a charge that can't be converted is refunded.
steps.decisionPrompt: The user pays, but their hold expired one second earlier. What happens to their money and the seat?

concepts.definition: Atomically turning a held seat into a sold one once payment succeeds.
concepts.whyItMatters: The seat must not be resold between charge and confirmation.

concepts.definition: Refunding/reversing if a step fails after money moved.
concepts.whyItMatters: Payment and inventory are separate systems; a saga handles partial failure.

flows.name: Pay-then-expire race
flows.note: Re-verify the hold inside the conversion; if expired after charge, compensate.
diagram: K->>I: convert held->sold (CAS on hold still active)
alt seat still held by this hold

traps.trap: Charging before confirming the hold is still valid
traps.why: The hold may have expired and the seat resold — you took money for nothing.
traps.instead: Re-verify inside the atomic conversion; if it lost the seat after charging, refund (compensate).
Idempotency key on checkout; the payment call is itself idempotent.

failureDrills.scenario: Payment succeeds but the seat conversion fails (hold expired in the gap).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: User isn't charged for a seat they didn't get.
failureDrills.mitigation: Saga compensation refunds; ideally extend/renew the hold for the payment window to shrink the race.

strong: Re-verifies the hold atomically with conversion and compensates on loss.
Makes checkout idempotent end to end.

recap.before: Holds didn't become real, paid orders.
recap.after: Holds convert to confirmed orders with idempotent, compensatable payment.
recap.newRisk: The charge↔conversion gap is a race — shrink it and compensate.

whyNow: A hold is worthless until it safely becomes a paid order without losing money or the seat.

steps.title: 7. Overbooking Prevention & Consistency
steps.description: Make 'never oversell' a hard invariant even across the lock store, inventory, and general-admission counters. Use the seat status as the single source of truth with optimistic concurrency; for GA, use an atomic counter that can never go negative.
steps.decisionPrompt: Your Redis lock store and your inventory DB disagree about seat A12. Which wins, and how do you prevent overselling?

concepts.definition: Inventory status (in the DB) is authoritative; the lock store is an accelerator.
concepts.whyItMatters: If two systems can disagree, one must be canonical or you oversell.

concepts.definition: A decrement-if-positive on a remaining-count for general admission.
concepts.whyItMatters: Bounds GA sales without per-seat rows; must be atomic to avoid going negative.

deepDives.title: Making oversell impossible
points: Inventory status with a version is the source of truth; the conversion is a compare-and-set on (available/held -> sold).
The lock store only gates who attempts the CAS — if it and the DB disagree, the DB wins.
For GA, decrement-if-positive an atomic counter; reject at zero. Reconcile counter vs. orders periodically.

traps.trap: Treating the lock store as the source of truth for sold/available
traps.why: A lock-store failure or eviction then lets the same seat sell twice.
traps.instead: The lock only gates the attempt; the authoritative sold/available transition is a CAS on inventory.

traps.trap: Non-atomic GA decrement (read count, then write count-1)
traps.why: Concurrent buyers both read N and both write N-1 — oversell.
traps.instead: Atomic decrement-if-positive; reject when it hits zero.

failureDrills.scenario: The lock store is wiped (eviction/restart) mid-sale.
failureDrills.mitigation: Locks are advisory; the inventory CAS is the real guard, so a lost lock at worst causes extra 409s, never an oversell.

strong: Names inventory CAS as authoritative and the lock as an accelerator.
Uses an atomic, non-negative counter for GA and reconciles it.
weak: Relies on the lock store for correctness.
Does read-modify-write on a GA count.

recap.before: Correctness leaned on the lock store staying perfect.
recap.after: Oversell is impossible by construction — the DB CAS / atomic counter is the guard.
recap.newRisk: More 409s under contention, which the waiting room and retries absorb.

whyNow: Before scaling out, the no-oversell invariant must hold even when components fail.

steps.title: 8. Scaling Hot Events & Anti-Abuse
steps.description: A handful of mega-events drive nearly all contention. Shard inventory by event so one hot drop is isolated, scale its waiting room and reserve path independently, and add bot/scalper defenses so fairness survives adversaries.
steps.decisionPrompt: One stadium tour is generating 95% of your load. How do you keep it from taking down every other event?

concepts.definition: Partitioning inventory, locks, and queues by event_id.
concepts.whyItMatters: Isolates a hot event's contention so it can't starve the rest of the platform.

concepts.definition: Bot detection, rate limits, and identity checks at the queue and reserve edges.
concepts.whyItMatters: Scalpers automate to beat fairness; defenses are part of correctness here.

options.name: Dedicated shard + autoscaled lane per hot event (default)
pros: A mega-event's contention is isolated; other events are unaffected.
Its waiting room and reserve capacity scale independently.
cons: Routing and per-event capacity planning add operational work.

options.name: One shared pool for all events
cons: One mega-drop's contention degrades every other event.

deepDives.title: Bot and scalper defense
points: Rate-limit and fingerprint at the waiting-room edge before tokens are issued.
Require verified identity / payment pre-auth for the hottest events; cap tickets per identity.
Feed abuse signals into admission so suspected bots get lower priority, not the front of the line.

traps.trap: One shared inventory/lock tier for all events
traps.why: A single mega-event's lock contention and queue load degrade unrelated events.
traps.instead: Shard by event so blast radius is contained; give hot events their own lane.

traps.trap: Treating bots as a post-hoc cleanup
traps.why: By the time you cancel scalper orders, real fans are gone and trust is damaged.
traps.instead: Defend at the edge (queue/reserve) so fairness holds during the sale, not after.

strong: Isolates hot events by sharding and dedicated lanes.
Builds anti-abuse into admission, not as cleanup.
weak: Shares one tier across all events.

recap.before: All events shared one contended tier; bots gamed fairness.
recap.after: Hot events are isolated and scaled independently with edge anti-abuse.
recap.newRisk: Routing, hot-event promotion, and abuse tuning are ongoing operational work.

whyNow: At platform scale, a few mega-events dominate load and adversaries actively attack fairness.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: CDN + read model for browse; anti-abuse screens clients before the virtual waiting room issues admission tokens; admitted, token-bearing reservations route per event to atomic seat locking with TTL holds; an expiry worker releases abandoned holds back to authoritative inventory; idempotent checkout converts held seats to sold via inventory CAS and charges payment through a compensating saga; per-event sharding (inventory and locks) isolates hot drops.

functional.requirement: Browse events with a live seat map
functional.how: Static map on CDN, availability from a read model/cache.

functional.requirement: Reserve specific seats with a hold
functional.how: Atomic multi-seat locking creates a short-lived hold.
Idempotent checkout charges payment and converts held->sold atomically.
TTL holds reaped by an expiry worker plus lazy checks.
Per-seat status for reserved; atomic non-negative counter for GA.

nonFunctional.how: Atomic seat locking plus an authoritative inventory CAS / GA counter.
Virtual waiting room admits in position order at a metered rate.
Admission control bounds core load; per-event sharding isolates hot drops.
Reads served from CDN and cache, off the contended primary.
Edge anti-abuse and per-identity caps feeding admission.
Shed/queue load rather than oversell; locks and holds expire safely.

say: Restate it: fixed scarce inventory meets a flash crowd; correctness and fairness dominate.
List functional (browse, reserve, checkout, expiry) and non-functional (no oversell, fairness, extreme concurrency).
Say the headline: this is a contention problem, not a throughput problem.
Size buyers-per-seat at on-sale, reserve attempts/s, and hold duration.
Sketch GET seatmap (cached), POST reservations (atomic), checkout, and the queue token.
Start naive to expose the double-sell race, then add atomic seat locking and TTL holds.
Add the waiting room for fairness/overload, then checkout+payment, then the no-oversell invariant.
Go deep where pushed: locking vs DB-CAS, hold expiry, or waiting-room fairness.
Shard by event to isolate hot drops; add edge anti-abuse for scalpers.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the dominant risk (contention on a hot seat).

expectations: Recognizes the double-sell race and reaches for a lock or atomic update.
Knows holds need a timeout so abandoned seats come back.
Designs atomic multi-seat holds with ordered locking and TTLs.
Adds a waiting room for fairness and overload protection.
Handles the pay-then-expire race in checkout with compensation.
Makes no-oversell a hard invariant with an authoritative CAS, treating the lock store as an accelerator.
Isolates hot events via sharding and dedicated lanes; reasons about blast radius.
Builds anti-abuse into admission and quantifies fairness vs. throughput tradeoffs.

followUps: How do you handle seat selection for groups who want adjacent seats under contention?
How would you implement dynamic pricing or auctions without breaking the no-oversell guarantee?
How do you support resale/transfer of tickets while preventing fraud and double-use at the gate?
How would you roll out presales and access codes that gate a subset of inventory?
What's your strategy when a hot event's dedicated shard still saturates?
How do you reconcile the lock store, inventory, and orders after a partial outage during a sale?
